fields.label: Sleeve Head/Shoulder Style

options.label: Natural
options.description: A style popularized by Brooks Brothers and mimicked by other American tailors, it is also known as the American shoulder. It was a signature detail of the Brooks Brothers “sack suit”. The sack suit, developed as a counterpoise to the highly structured suits that reigned supreme in Europe, was the de facto American business suit for decades.

options.label: THE STRUCTURED / ENGLISH SHOULDER
options.description: The English suit is the most structured of all suits, featuring large padding and significant canvasing, it is also interlined with horsetail and reinforced with chest felt and tapes.  The shoulder line is extended and roped (save for a few exceptions such as Anderson & Sheppard) creating an almost martial appearance. Regular

options.label: Con Rollino/ THE CONTINENTAL SHOULDER
options.description: Featuring a refined Con Rollino shoulder, this jacket delivers soft Italian tailoring with a subtle raised sleevehead for a sophisticated, natural drape and effortless elegance.

options.label: Neapolitan (Spalla Camicia)
options.description: The Continental shoulder is lightly padded but follows a strong, straight line from the collar down to the edge of the shoulders, and is finished with a medium amount of roping.

options.label: Shirt Head/THE UNSTRUCTURED
options.description: THE UNSTRUCTURED / ITALIAN SHOULDER The shoulder styles on this kind of jacket are referred to as spalla a camicia, i.e. “like a shirt” in Italian. This construction is designed to drape naturally over the shoulders and not alter a man’s shoulder line. Only a thin layer of canvas is used in the Italian suit. The fit, drape and small arm hole are meant to allow a free range of motion.  Usually worn to events with a relaxed code of dress where one would still want to look stylish, the Italian shoulder confers nonchalance but in an elegant, casual way.

fields.hint: The sleeve head and shoulder construction are among the most defining elements of a bespoke jacket, shaping not only its silhouette but also its comfort, drape, and overall character. From structured British shoulders to soft Italian expressions like the Con Rollino, each style creates a distinct visual impression and wearing experience. Choosing the right shoulder and sleeve head allows a bespoke garment to complement the wearer’s physique, posture, and personal aesthetic with precision.

fields.label: Canvas Construction

options.label: Regular Fused
options.description: Fused construction bonds a synthetic interlining to the back of the outer fabric using heat and adhesive, creating immediate structure without any hand-stitching. The result is a jacket that feels uniform and stiff from the first wear, with flat lapels that lack the gentle roll of a canvassed garment. Over time — especially with dry-cleaning — the glue degrades and the interlining separates from the face cloth, causing the characteristic bubbling delamination that ruins a fused jacket irreparably.

options.label: Ultra-thin Half Canvas
options.description: Ultra-thin half canvas uses an exceptionally lightweight horsehair and linen canvas restricted to the chest and lapel zone, approaching the featherweight territory of Neapolitan unstructured tailoring while retaining a soft chest shape. The jacket drapes with a whisper of form, moving almost like a shirt on the body, making it ideal for tropical and super-lightweight fabrics where any additional mass would be suffocating. The chest maintains a subtle convex curve but the overall silhouette is deliberately understated and fluid.

options.label: Light Half Canvas
options.description: Light half canvas uses a reduced-weight horsehair canvas from shoulder to mid-chest, offering noticeably more drape and softness than standard half canvas while still preserving a clean chest architecture and a lapel with genuine roll. It is the preferred construction for medium-weight fabrics in warmer climates, threading the needle between structured formality and easy wearability. The jacket breaks in gracefully and moulds gently to the torso over time without the full conforming memory of a true full-floating canvas.

options.label: Regular Half Canvas
options.description: Standard half canvas is the workhorse of quality tailoring: a horsehair and linen canvas running from the shoulder seam down to roughly the mid-chest, hand-padstitched through the lapels to create their characteristic rolling break. Below the canvas, the lower body is stabilised with a light fused interlining, striking a practical balance between genuine craft and production economy. The result is a jacket that drapes naturally at the chest and lapel — where 80% of the visual impression is formed — and holds a crisper, more dimensional front than any fully fused garment.

options.label: Full Canvas (Floating)
options.description: Full floating canvas is the gold standard of bespoke construction: a continuous layer of horsehair, wool, and linen canvas runs from the shoulder all the way to the hem of the jacket, loosely hand-padstitched so it floats freely between the outer cloth and the lining. With each wearing the canvas slowly memorises the curves and posture of its owner, creating a chest swell and a drape that is biologically unique to the wearer. The lapels roll from the second button in a continuous, organic arc rather than breaking at a sharp crease — the true hallmark of an heirloom garment.

options.label: Quarter Canvas
options.description: Quarter canvas places a small canvas chest piece only at the very upper chest — roughly the top quarter of the front panel — providing a floating lapel with natural roll while the remainder of the jacket body is stabilised with a light fuse. It delivers the most important single benefit of canvassed construction at minimum cost, and sits above a true fused jacket in quality and feel, though without the chest-shaping properties that a full half-canvas provides. Quarter canvas is common in well-made prêt-à-porter garments positioned above entry-level fused construction.

fields.hint: Canvas construction is the foundation of a bespoke jacket, determining how the garment molds to the body, maintains its shape, and evolves over time. Unlike fused garments, a properly crafted canvas allows the jacket to breathe, drape naturally, and develop a personalized fit unique to the wearer. From full canvas to half canvas variations, each construction method offers a different balance of structure, softness, durability, and comfort.

options.label: No Padding
options.description: No padding produces the most natural, body-following shoulder possible — the sleeve is set into the armhole so the jacket falls directly from the wearer's natural shoulder point with no added projection or squaring. Associated above all with Neapolitan tailoring and the spalla camicia (shirt shoulder) tradition, this construction gives the jacket an almost relaxed, casually aristocratic quality: soft, human, and free of any militaristic squareness. It demands a clean natural shoulder from the wearer to look its best, but rewards one with a silhouette of exceptional ease and grace.

options.label: +0.1 cm (Both Sides)
options.description: A 1mm padding addition is essentially imperceptible to the naked eye but just enough to square the shoulder point by a fraction and prevent any slight hollowing that occurs when a jacket hangs without support at the sleeve head. It is the preference of tailors who value the natural Italian look but wish to provide a tiny insurance against an imperfect shoulder line, creating a finish that photographs as clean and natural but sits slightly more defined in person. This is the most subtle intervention in the spectrum of shoulder construction — barely there, simply ensuring the garment has an impeccable, defined edge.

options.label: +0.3 cm (Both Sides)
options.description: A 3mm pad gives a gentle but visible lift to the shoulder line, creating a slightly cleaner horizontal projection without introducing any of the hard angularity of traditional British construction. This is the natural resting point for most bespoke clients: enough structure to project confidence and give the jacket a tailored rather than casual identity, but soft enough that the shoulder curves gently into the sleeve with no visible ridge. It pairs equally well with a natural rolled sleevehead or a more polished set sleeve, and is the standard padding weight across most Milanese and Roman tailoring houses.

options.label: +0.5 cm Soft (Both Sides)
options.description: A 5mm soft pad is the classical bespoke standard — substantial enough to project a definite, authoritative shoulder line while remaining visibly rounded and human rather than squared off. The soft construction means the pad compresses naturally under pressure and presents a gently curved profile that echoes the natural deltoid rather than fighting it, giving the jacket both presence and ease. This is the padding weight favoured by the great London houses for day suits: purposeful enough to command attention, comfortable enough to wear for twelve hours without fatigue.

options.label: +0.5 cm Structured (Both Sides)
options.description: A 5mm structured pad uses a firm, non-compressible pad material set with a pronounced roped sleevehead — the wadding is built up at the very edge of the shoulder to create a crisp, square projection that is the unmistakable signature of traditional British military tailoring. The roping raises the sleevehead above the shoulder line by a few millimetres, creating a visible ridge that draws the eye upward and outward, projecting power and formality. This construction is at home in a Savile Row business suit or morning coat and reads instantly as English, authoritative, and deliberately architectural.

options.label: Notch 45° (Semi-notch)
options.description: The 45-degree semi-notch lapel sits at an unusually low, almost horizontal angle where the collar meets the lapel, producing a relaxed, casual character that softens the formality of any jacket. This very shallow cut draws the eye inward toward the chest with a gentle, unhurried line, giving single-breasted suits a relaxed, Continental ease. It is associated with soft-constructed Neapolitan tailoring and works best on unstructured or lightly structured jackets intended for day or leisure wear.

options.label: Notch 50° (BC)
options.description: The 50-degree BC notch represents a classic British cut interpretation of the notch lapel, with a slightly steeper angle than the semi-notch yet still retaining a traditional, unhurried elegance. The V of the notch forms cleanly and evenly, producing a symmetrical, authoritative line across the chest that reads as polished without any sense of aggression. This lapel is a reliable workhorse for business suits — universally appropriate, easy to proportion, and flattering across a wide range of body types.

options.label: Notch 55°
options.description: The 55-degree notch is the canonical standard of the modern suit jacket — the geometric midpoint that has dominated tailoring for generations. The notch opens cleanly at a 55-degree angle to the gorge seam, balancing the breadth of the chest against the height of the collar and creating a look that reads as both contemporary and timeless. This is the lapel that defines the business suit in the collective imagination, equally at home in a boardroom or at a formal occasion that falls short of black tie.

options.label: Notch 65°
options.description: The 65-degree high-gorge notch places the break point closer to the shoulder, producing a notch that reads as smart, modern, and intentional. The higher placement elongates the perceived length of the lapel run, creating a leaner, more vertical visual line that flatters most silhouettes and reads as contemporary business dress. This is the gorge position preferred by many Italian and British bespoke houses today, as it gives a single-breasted suit a sharper, more dressed quality without committing to the formality of a peak lapel.

options.label: Notch 68° (Curved Gorge)
options.description: The curved gorge notch at 68 degrees is a distinctly Italian refinement in which the gorge seam follows a gentle curve rather than a straight line, softening what would otherwise be a sharp mechanical angle into something more sculptural and alive. Associated with Florentine and Milanese ateliers, this lapel style communicates craftsmanship and intentionality — the curve is invisible to a casual observer but signals to the trained eye that the jacket was made with unusual care. The overall effect is a notch lapel that appears to melt naturally into the collar rather than simply joining it.

options.label: Straight Notch 73°
options.description: The straight notch at 73 degrees is cut at a high and steep angle, creating a pronounced, near-vertical open V at the gorge that delivers a bold, contemporary character to an otherwise classic lapel family. This high placement creates maximum visible collar space above the notch and gives the jacket a long-lined, fashion-forward energy that reads as modern tailoring rather than tradition. It bridges the visual gap between the standard notch and the peak lapel, adding assertiveness without the full directional aggression of a peak.

options.label: Notch + Pointed Tab
options.description: The notch lapel with a pointed tab is a rare bespoke detail in which a small triangular point of fabric bridges across the gorge opening, creating a decorative closure that recalls the military and hunting-coat origins of the modern suit lapel. The pointed tab adds an element of precision and character to an otherwise conventional notch, and when pressed flat it creates a clean, horizontal line that emphasizes the breadth of the chest. It is a sartorial flourish best suited to clients with a taste for historical menswear references and quiet eccentricity.

options.label: Notch + Basic Tab
options.description: The notch lapel with a basic straight tab is a cleaner, more restrained version of the tabbed notch, in which a flat rectangular piece of fabric spans the gorge in a horizontal bridge. The effect is structural rather than ornamental — it closes the V-shaped opening with a crisp line that gives the upper chest a finished, tailored quality that the open notch cannot achieve. Often seen on tweed, flannel, and country-wear jackets, the tab lapel is a mark of traditional English tailoring heritage and carries associations with sporting dress and the British countryside.

options.label: Peak 99°
options.description: The 99-degree peak lapel is the most restrained entry point into peak territory — the upward-pointing tip barely crosses the horizontal, producing a lapel that reads as a peak only on close inspection. This subtlety makes it an ideal choice for clients who want the broadening, shoulder-enhancing effect of the peak without the formality or drama it usually implies. It works exceptionally well on single-breasted business suits where the wearer wants a distinctive silhouette that remains appropriate across all professional contexts.

options.label: Peak 101°
options.description: The 101-degree peak lapel crosses the horizontal plane just slightly further than the 99-degree variant, establishing an unmistakable but still restrained peak that signals intention without announcement. The tip points outward and upward with quiet authority, creating a horizontal emphasis at the chest that broadens the shoulder line and adds structure to the overall silhouette. This is a versatile business-and-event lapel, one that works on single-breasted suits destined for both office and dinner table.

options.label: Peak 102°
options.description: The 102-degree peak is the archetypal classic sharp peak — the lapel form that defines the single-breasted peak suit in the popular imagination. The tip points clearly outward and upward, creating a crisp triangular wedge that anchors the collar with visual decisiveness and draws the eye across the breadth of the chest. It reads as unambiguously formal and confident, elevating a single-breasted suit to event-wear territory while remaining appropriate for high-level business dress.

options.label: Peak 102° RL
options.description: The RL-cut peak at 102 degrees is the same classic peak angle executed with the RL (right-left) cut geometry, a subtle variation in how the collar and lapel panel seam is drafted that slightly adjusts the opening width and the visual relationship of the gorge to the lapel face. The overall impression is a sharp, authoritative classic peak but with a proprietary character visible to connoisseurs, producing a chest line that is marginally more open or closed depending on the specific cut house. It is the equivalent of a house signature within the peak lapel family.

options.label: Curved Peak 103°
options.description: The curved 103-degree peak lapel is an Italian bespoke refinement in which the straight edge of the peak is replaced by a gently concave or convex curve, lending the lapel an organic, sculptural quality quite unlike the hard geometry of a standard peak. This treatment, associated with Florentine houses like Liverano & Liverano and certain Neapolitan ateliers, softens what would otherwise be an aggressive angle and creates a lapel that appears to have grown naturally from the collar. The result is simultaneously more elegant and more directional than a straight peak of equivalent angle.

options.label: Peak 105°
options.description: At 105 degrees the peak lapel takes on a notably assertive character, with the tip extending clearly outward toward the shoulder seam and creating a strong horizontal visual anchor at the chest. This is the classic peak angle associated with mid-20th-century power dressing and the single-breasted suits of Savile Row's golden age — it projects authority and confidence without straying into exaggeration. The overall effect on the jacket is to broaden the shoulders visually, compress the waist, and create a decisively masculine silhouette.

options.label: Peak 107° (Normal Curve)
options.description: The 107-degree peak with a normal curve represents the standard wide-peak silhouette of the classic double-breasted suit, where the lapel extends well past the horizontal and curves gently toward the armhole. This is the traditional form associated with English double-breasted tailoring, Hollywood suiting of the 1940s, and the power suits of Milanese designers in the 1980s revival. The width and angle together create a jacket front of great visual presence — broadened shoulders, deep chest, commanding overall scale.

options.label: Straight Peak 108°
options.description: The straight peak at 108 degrees is a dramatic, architectural lapel cut that dispenses entirely with any softening curve and presents the peak tip as a sharp, uncompromising geometric point angled directly toward the shoulder. The straight edge running from the gorge to the tip creates a hard triangular form with maximum visual impact, most at home on double-breasted jackets and formal evening wear where strong silhouette is a virtue. This is the lapel of choice for clients who want their tailoring to project strength and precision above all else.

options.label: Peak 110°
options.description: The 110-degree peak lapel sits at the extreme end of classic peak geometry, producing a tip that points nearly horizontally outward toward the shoulder with maximum breadth and visual impact. This is the lapel of grand occasion — of opera, of state dinners, of the most formal single- and double-breasted evening suits — where the scale and drama of the peak are not merely permitted but expected. In the hands of a skilled bespoke cutter, a peak of this angle can be balanced to look commanding rather than theatrical.

options.label: Low Peak 110°
options.description: The low peak at 110 degrees combines the wide, sweeping angle of a 110-degree peak with a gorge placement set deliberately lower on the chest, producing a lapel of unusual breadth and horizontal authority. The low gorge means the peak runs longer before breaking to the tip, creating an expansive chest panel and a decidedly vintage, 1940s-inspired sensibility. This is the lapel of the Hollywood suit — wide-shouldered, deep-chested, cinematic — and it rewards wearers with the frame to carry its scale.

options.label: Peak 114° (Semi-peak)
options.description: The 114-degree semi-peak occupies the transitional zone between the conventional peak and the nearly flat wide-peak, where the tip is so near horizontal that it creates an almost graphic, billboard-like chest statement. At this angle the lapel's tip points predominantly outward with only a very slight upward deflection, producing an effect that is more about breadth than height — a powerful horizontal bar across the chest that maximizes apparent shoulder width. This is a bold, fashion-forward choice that reads best on broad-shouldered, tall wearers and structured, high-armhole jackets.

options.label: Peak 115°
options.description: At 115 degrees the peak tip points almost exactly horizontal or fractionally below it, creating a lapel of extraordinary breadth that pushes the silhouette toward the theatrical and spectacular. This extreme angle is rarely seen in contemporary ready-to-wear but persists in the bespoke traditions of certain Italian and South American tailors who specialize in dramatic, show-stopping evening and occasion suits. It produces a jacket front of remarkable visual presence and should only be considered for clients whose personality and occasion can absorb its considerable declaration.

options.label: Curved Peak 120°
options.description: The curved 120-degree peak represents the absolute outer limit of the peak lapel family — an extreme, sweeping form in which the tip describes a graceful arc well past the horizontal and the edge curves rather than cuts straight to the point. This is the lapel of the grande tailleur, of fashion-week suiting and operatic ceremony — a form that prioritizes spectacle and artistry over any conventional definition of wearability. When executed in a fine fabric with immaculate tailoring, it creates a jacket of staggering visual drama that wears more like sculpture than clothing.

options.label: Shawl Lapel
options.description: The classic shawl lapel is the oldest and most formally distinguished of all lapel forms — a single continuous piece of fabric that runs from the collar around the lapel without any notch or peak, flowing in an unbroken curve from shoulder to button. Born from the Victorian smoking jacket, it has since become synonymous with the dinner jacket and tuxedo, communicating ease, elegance, and black-tie refinement. A shawl lapel suit says the wearer understands formality not as rigidity but as a kind of liberated ease.

options.label: D-Shawl Lapel
options.description: The D-shawl lapel is a variation of the classic shawl in which the outer curve of the lapel is rendered as a more pronounced D-shape rather than the usual gentle arc, giving the lapel a more geometric, graphic character that reads as distinctly modern. The D-profile creates a fuller, rounder lapel that sits higher and bolder on the chest, lending a dinner jacket a more fashion-forward and sculptural silhouette. It is frequently seen on velvet jackets and smoking suits where a more expressive form is welcome.

options.label: Shawl 0A
options.description: The Shawl 0A is a refined standard shawl lapel variant distinguished by its particular proportions and roll line — typically a moderately wide shawl with a gentle curve and medium roll that produces a composed, classic formal silhouette. This construction favors clean lines over drama and presents the wearer with a shawl lapel that communicates old-money understatement rather than fashion-forward novelty. It is the correct choice for a dinner jacket intended to serve across multiple occasions and decades.

options.label: Shawl 0E
options.description: The Shawl 0E is a narrower, more contemporary shawl variant with a tighter roll and reduced width across the chest, producing a sleek, modern dinner jacket silhouette that suits slim-built wearers and close-fitting single-button jackets. The reduced width gives it a fashionable restraint that reads as intentional minimalism rather than conservatism, making it particularly well-suited to coloured or textured evening jackets where the fabric itself provides the visual interest. It retains all the ease and elegance of the classic shawl while giving the jacket a leaner, more current character.

options.label: Shawl 0005
options.description: The Shawl 0005 is a bespoke house-specific variant of the shawl collar with its own particular point of origin in the roll, curve geometry, and facial width that distinguishes it subtly but meaningfully from the standard shawl. These micro-differences in the geometry of how the collar transitions into the lapel are the signature details that allow discerning clients to recognize the hand of a particular atelier. The visual result remains a classically smooth, notch-free formal lapel, but with a distinctive personality visible to the educated eye.

options.label: Asymmetric Shawl Collar
options.description: The asymmetric shawl collar is a radical avant-garde variation in which one lapel is shaped or positioned differently from the other — one side may be fuller, wider, or set at a different height — creating a deliberate imbalance that reads as a strong design statement. This form abandons the bilateral symmetry that governs all conventional tailoring and invites the viewer to look twice, making it the preserve of fashion designers, theatrical tailors, and clients who wish their clothes to function as art. When executed with precision it can appear strikingly elegant; in lesser hands it simply reads as a mistake.

options.label: Peak + Removable Shawl
options.description: The peak lapel with a removable shawl conversion is a bespoke engineering feat that allows a single jacket to present two entirely different formal personae: the assertive, structured peak lapel for daytime and semi-formal occasions, and the smooth, flowing shawl for black-tie events. The shawl element attaches over the peak — usually via hidden buttons or hand-worked hooks at the gorge — and when fitted gives no outward sign of the mechanism beneath. This is the ultimate expression of sartorial versatility, a garment that adapts to the occasion rather than the other way around.

options.label: Notch + Removable Shawl
options.description: The notch lapel with a removable shawl conversion extends the same dual-identity concept to the more relaxed notch lapel family, allowing a standard suit jacket to moonlight as a dinner jacket by attaching a shawl collar piece at the gorge. The result when the shawl is applied is a dinner jacket of conventional, smooth formality; when removed the jacket returns to everyday business suit territory. This construction is favoured by clients who attend formal occasions frequently enough to justify the engineering but not frequently enough to commission a dedicated tuxedo.

options.label: Italian Fishmouth Lapel
options.description: The Italian fishtail lapel — known in French tailoring as the cran Camps or cran parisien and associated strongly with the Parisian house Camps de Luca — is a lapel in which the underside of the tip curves slightly upward after the gorge, closing off the open space between lapel and collar and creating a strong horizontal line rather than the usual V-shaped opening. The result is a lapel that appears to grip the collar with intentional firmness, producing a distinctive fish-mouth profile that is simultaneously more formal than a standard notch and less aggressive than a peak. It is the signature lapel of Parisian grand tailoring, communicating supreme self-assurance and institutional knowledge.

options.label: None
options.description: Omitting the chest pocket entirely produces the cleanest, most austere front on a jacket, erasing any visual interruption across the chest. Historically associated with tailcoats and the most elevated forms of formalwear, it was considered the hallmark of true ceremonial dress. In modern bespoke tailoring it is chosen on double-breasted dinner suits or highly formal morning coats where a boutonnière replaces the pocket square as the only chest ornament.

options.label: Straight Welt 2.3 cm
options.description: A straight welt breast pocket at 2.3 cm is the narrowest conventional English welt, lending the chest a particularly refined, restrained line. The slim welt draws minimal attention to itself and reads as deliberate minimalism rather than economy, suiting dark worsted city suits and formal jackets where the pocket square should float freely above the opening. This width is especially popular with Savile Row firms who favour a long, lean chest line with tight, precise jetted work on the welt fabric itself.

options.label: Straight Welt 2.5 cm
options.description: At 2.5 cm the straight welt breast pocket represents the most widely used English standard, balancing visibility with formality. It sits precisely in the sweet spot between the ultra-narrow and the wider Italian welt, making it the default reference point for most British bespoke houses and many quality ready-to-wear makers. The width allows a generous display of pocket square while keeping the chest geometry clean and orderly.

options.label: Straight Welt 2.7 cm
options.description: A 2.7 cm straight welt is a deliberately wider reading of the English breast pocket, giving the chest a slightly bolder, more pronounced horizontal accent. It is often chosen on roomy hopsack or tweed sport jackets where a thicker welt is proportionally in keeping with a more textured cloth. The additional millimetres allow the pocket square to arch more generously upward, creating an immediately expressive display.

options.label: Curved Welt 2.3 cm
options.description: The curved welt at 2.3 cm — known in Italian tailoring as the barchetta, or 'little boat' — describes a gentle upward arc across the chest that follows the natural curvature of the pectoral muscle. At the narrowest barchetta width, the curve is subtle and refined, adding a whisper of Italian character without overwhelming the jacket's overall silhouette. It is favoured on structured Neapolitan suits where the curve reads as a sophisticated counterpoint to the clean lapel line.

options.label: Curved Welt 2.5 cm
options.description: The curved welt at 2.5 cm is the most common barchetta specification, placing it at the standard English welt width but with the Italian-inflected arc that makes it instantly recognisable. The combination of familiar width and distinctive curve gives this pocket wide appeal: it is at home on both city suits and sport jackets, and it is the specification most Italian tailors default to when a client asks for 'Neapolitan' character. The upward curve creates a natural shelf that showcases a folded pocket square to exceptional effect.

options.label: Curved Welt 2.7 cm
options.description: At 2.7 cm the curved welt becomes a statement in its own right: the wider arc sweeps more dramatically across the chest, emphasising breadth and giving the jacket a distinctly Italian generosity of character. This specification is typical of Neapolitan soft tailoring, where the chest is not heavily structured and the broader barchetta adds architectural interest in place of padding. It is an ideal choice on unlined or half-lined jackets in summer cloths where the pocket's curve echoes the soft, natural roll of the lapels.

options.label: Curved Welt 2.9 cm
options.description: A 2.9 cm curved welt is the widest barchetta commonly specified in bespoke tailoring, producing a pronounced boat-shaped opening that becomes a focal element of the jacket's front. This specification originated in the boldest Neapolitan ateliers and is best suited to roomy, softly constructed jackets in substantial cloths — tweed, flannel, or heavy hopsack — where a wide pocket feels in scale. The generous curve creates an almost sculptural effect across the chest, and a carefully puffed pocket square in this configuration reads like a piece of considered artistry.

options.label: Jetted Chest Pocket
options.description: The double-jetted, or besom, breast pocket presents only two thin parallel strips of cloth flanking a neat slit — with no welt standing above the opening. This is the most formal of all chest pocket treatments, de rigueur on black tie dinner jackets and white tie coats, where any additional fabric would disturb the polished, uninterrupted front. The parallel jets must be cut with meticulous precision: their width should be identical, their edges perfectly parallel, and the corners mitered with surgical accuracy — qualities that distinguish a master tailor's work.

options.label: Patch Pocket
options.description: A patch breast pocket is a separate piece of fabric sewn directly onto the chest of the jacket with its top edge left open, creating a visible, three-dimensional pocket. It is the most casual breast pocket treatment and signals a relaxed, sporting character — most at home on unlined tweed jackets, hopsack blazers, and country wear. Because it sits proud of the jacket front rather than being cut into it, it has a tactile, handmade quality prized in soft Neapolitan and unconstructed tailoring.

options.label: Round Patch
options.description: The round-bottomed patch breast pocket softens the geometry of the jacket front by replacing square-cut corners with a continuous curve that sweeps around the pocket's lower edge. This curvaceous shape is a hallmark of Neapolitan tailoring and echoes the rounded patch pockets sometimes used at the hip, giving the jacket a coherent, relaxed Italian personality. It is the natural choice on soft, unconstructed jackets in linen or open-weave cloth where angular details would look out of character.

options.label: Tulip Shape Patch
options.description: The tulip-shaped patch chest pocket is a design flourish in which the two upper corners of the pocket flare outward in gentle curves before turning down, evoking the silhouette of an open tulip bloom. It is an uncommon, high-individuality choice found primarily in the portfolios of creative Neapolitan and Florentine tailors who wish to give a jacket a distinctive artisanal signature. The organic form softens the jacket's front considerably and works best on fluid, unlined jackets where the pocket can be cut with relaxed, almost sculptural freedom.

options.label: Angled Box Pleat Patch
options.description: The angled box-pleat patch pocket combines an angular, typically asymmetric upper edge — cut on a rake that mirrors the jacket's natural chest angle — with a central box pleat running down the face of the patch to allow the pocket to open and carry a square with ease. The angled cut prevents the pocket from reading as flat or uniform, and the box pleat adds gentle volume and a subtle country-sporting quality. It is particularly well suited to shooting and hacking jackets in tweed or heavy worsted where functionality and rugged elegance are equally prized.

options.label: Multi-Pleated Patch
options.description: The multi-pleated patch chest pocket features two or more pleats — inverted or box — running vertically across its face, giving the pocket an accordion-like capacity to expand beyond its flat dimensions. This is the most utilitarian and visually busy of the chest patch pocket options, with deep roots in military and expedition clothing. In bespoke tailoring it appears on elaborate field jackets, safari-influenced designs, and highly individual commissions for clients who prioritise both character and practicality.

options.label: Boat-shaped 2.8 cm
options.description: The boat-shaped breast pocket at 2.8 cm refers to a welt pocket whose opening traces a shallow crescent — wider at the centre and rising toward both ends — that closely resembles the hull of a small dinghy. At 2.8 cm it is a confidently sized barchetta, typically associated with the boldest Neapolitan tailors who want the pocket to be an expressive design element rather than a quiet utility feature. The curved form follows the chest's natural contour and is considered by many connoisseurs to be the most flattering breast pocket shape for displaying a pocket square.

options.label: Boat-shaped 3.0 cm
options.description: At 3.0 cm the boat-shaped breast pocket reaches a generous, full-bodied dimension that transforms it into one of the most visually commanding details on the jacket's front. This is the widest barchetta regularly specified, associated with the most expressive and relaxed Italian tailoring traditions — particularly soft, deconstructed Neapolitan jackets in substantial cloths. The extra width means the curvature of the welt sweeps over a broader arc, making it visible even at a distance and serving as the defining design feature of the jacket.

options.label: Trapezoid Pocket
options.description: The trapezoid chest pocket departs from both the rectangle and the arc by cutting the welt or pocket opening as a trapezoid — wider at the top than the bottom — producing a shape that tapers as it descends and creates a subtle visual wedge effect on the chest. This creates a quiet visual wedge effect that can slim and elongate the torso, and is associated with avant-garde or highly individualistic bespoke commissions. It is an uncommon feature that functions as a quiet signature for tailors who enjoy subverting conventional geometry.

options.label: Two Inverted Pleated + Flaps
options.description: This unusual chest pocket combines an inverted pleat running down the centre of the patch — allowing the pocket to expand outward when loaded — with two flanking flaps that frame the opening on either side, creating an articulated, almost architectural appearance on the chest. It is a sporting and highly individual option, more often found on elaborate country or shooting jackets than on city suits. The construction demands considerable skill and signals a client comfortable with visible tailoring craft.

options.label: Inverted Pleated + Flap
options.description: An inverted-pleat patch breast pocket topped by a single flap is a classic Savile Row country-wear detail: the central inverted pleat allows the pocket to billow outward and hold a pocket square or notes with ease, while the flap adds a neat horizontal closure above the opening. It reads as sporting and purposeful without the visual complexity of twin flaps, and is frequently seen on tweed jackets, hacking coats, and stalking jackets where the pocket must be as practical as it is elegant.

options.label: Slanted Flap 4.0 cm
options.description: The slanted flap pocket — also known as the hacking pocket — is the single most recognisable detail in British bespoke tailoring, its angled cut tracing the line from the jacket's front edge upward toward the side seam at roughly 15–20 degrees. The rake has equestrian origins: riders on horseback could slip a hand into the pocket without unbuttoning or fumbling at the hip. On a city suit the angled flap adds a dynamic, English quality that differentiates the jacket from Continental competitors, and when the flap is tucked in it presents as a clean slanted jetted pocket.

options.label: Large Slanted Flap 4.0 cm
options.description: The large slanted flap is the same hacking-style pocket cut at a steeper angle or with a noticeably wider and deeper flap, creating a more emphatic visual statement on the hip. It is associated with tweed country jackets, overcoats, and double-breasted suits where a commanding lower pocket anchors the jacket's weight and presence. The scale suits taller clients or those wearing voluminous cloth — flannel, tweed, or heavy barathea — where a standard-width flap would look pinched or understated.

options.label: Straight Jetted Flap 4.0 cm
options.description: The straight jetted flap pocket runs horizontally across the hip and is covered by a flat, rectangular flap that lies flush with the jacket face — the most common lower pocket treatment on business and city suits worldwide. The horizontal orientation is more formal than the slanted hacking pocket and reads as clean and authoritative, suiting all occasions from the boardroom to black-tie dinner. The flap can be tucked in to reveal a pure besom pocket underneath, offering the wearer flexibility in how formal they wish to appear.

options.label: Straight Jetted Pocket 4 cm
options.description: The jetted, or besom, lower pocket presents only two precisely cut strips of cloth — the jets — above and below a neat horizontal slit, with no flap to interrupt the jacket's surface. It is the most formal hip pocket treatment, mandatory on dinner jackets and tuxedos, where it reinforces the sleek, uncluttered front that black tie demands. In bespoke work the jets are the ultimate test of a cutter's precision: they must be the exact same width along their full length, finish at identical points at both ends, and lie perfectly flat without drawing or puckering.

options.label: Straight Jetted Pocket
options.description: The straight jetted flap pocket runs horizontally across the hip and is covered by a flat, rectangular flap that lies flush with the jacket face — the most common lower pocket treatment on business and city suits worldwide. options.label: Patch Pocket
options.description: The patch lower pocket is a piece of cloth applied directly to the outside of the jacket over the hip, with its top edge open and all other edges topstitched or hand-stitched to the face cloth. It is the most casual hip pocket option, instantly signalling a relaxed, sporting jacket rather than a formal suit — it is at home on hopsack blazers, tweed sport coats, and Neapolitan soft jackets but is never appropriate on a city business suit. The patch pocket gives a jacket an artisanal, almost improvised quality that has been prized in Italian tailoring since the postwar years.

options.label: Rounded Patch
options.description: The rounded lower patch pocket — called tasca a pignata by Neapolitan tailors — replaces the square corners of a standard patch pocket with a continuous curved lower edge and gently rounded corners. The curvature is wholly characteristic of Neapolitan tailoring, complementing the rounded cutaway fronts and soft construction those houses favour. A jacket with rounded patch pockets at the hip and a barchetta breast pocket is one of the most recognisable expressions of the Neapolitan canon.

options.label: Patch with Basic Flap
options.description: The patch-with-flap combines the external, sewn-on construction of the patch pocket with an additional flap that folds over the opening to create a covered closure — a combination that is a cornerstone of American Ivy Style and traditional English country tailoring alike. It is more functional than an open patch and slightly less casual in appearance, remaining firmly in the relaxed category appropriate for sport coats, tweed jackets, and unlined blazers. The flap typically matches the jacket cloth and may be butted to the pocket or slightly overhanging.

options.label: Inverted Pleated + Basic Flap
options.description: The inverted-pleat lower pocket with flap sits at the intersection of style and function: a central inverted pleat expands the patch as the pocket fills, providing significantly more carrying volume than a flat patch, while a flap closes over the top to keep contents secure and present a neat, traditional silhouette. It is a country-sporting detail associated with shooting jackets, stalking coats, and heavy-duty tweed commissions where the wearer genuinely needs to carry items in the pocket without distorting the jacket's hang.

options.label: Inverted Pleated + Flap & Button
options.description: Pleated + flap + button.

options.label: Water-drop Shape Patch
options.description: The water-drop shaped lower patch pocket is an exceedingly rare, avant-garde construction in which the patch is cut to resemble a teardrop: the upper portion is narrow and upright, widening gradually toward a rounded lower body. This creates a distinctive organic silhouette on the hip that references natural forms rather than geometric tailoring conventions. It is found almost exclusively on highly individual bespoke commissions from creative Italian or contemporary tailors who treat the jacket as a canvas for sculptural expression.

options.label: None
options.description: Omitting the bartack at the pocket corner leaves the junction between pocket opening and face cloth without any additional reinforcement stitch, producing the cleanest possible finish at the pocket mouth with no ornamental stitch visible to interrupt the cloth. This is appropriate on the most minimalist bespoke work where every surface detail has been considered and found unnecessary. In practice, fine bespoke construction relies on the strength of the underlying hand-stitching to secure pocket edges without a visible bartack.

options.label: D-Bartack
options.description: The D-shaped bartack is a half-moon or D-profile reinforcement stitch placed at the top corners of a pocket opening, its curved outer edge following the stress line where the pocket mouth meets the face cloth. The D-form distributes tension across a larger arc than a linear bar tack, making it particularly effective on heavier cloths and working pockets subject to repeated use. In bespoke tailoring it is regarded as a refined functional detail that, because of its decorative curved shape, is visible and intentional without looking merely utilitarian.

options.label: I-Bartack
options.description: The I-bartack is a straight, vertical or horizontal bar of dense stitching — literally shaped like the letter I — applied at each end of a pocket opening to lock the jets or welt in place and prevent the opening from spreading. It is one of the oldest and most traditional bartack forms in tailoring, closely associated with English bespoke work, and its simplicity makes it nearly invisible at conversational distance while providing robust reinforcement at the most stressed points of the pocket construction.

options.label: X-Bartack
options.description: The X-bartack places an X-shaped cross of reinforcement stitching at each pocket corner, with the four legs of the cross radiating outward to anchor the pocket opening across multiple stress vectors simultaneously. This form is visually the most expressive of the conventional bartack options and has a long heritage in both tailoring and workwear where it appears on pocket corners and seam intersections that require maximum durability. On a bespoke suit it reads as a considered, artisanal flourish — a small mark of craft that rewards close inspection.

options.label: 1-Bartack
options.description: The arrow bartack — also called an arrowhead tack — is a triangular reinforcement stitch hand-sewn at each pocket corner, its point directed toward the stress zone and its body filling a compact triangular area with dense stitching. It is one of the most beautiful of all reinforcement stitches and has a strong association with Savile Row and high Edwardian tailoring, where it was applied by hand using silk thread. The arrow bartack is at once a functional anchor and a signature of skilled hand tailoring, rewarding those who look closely with the evidence of genuine craft.

options.label: None
options.description: No ticket pocket.

options.label: Jetted Ticket Pocket
options.description: The jetted ticket pocket is a small besom — two slim jet strips flanking a horizontal slit — positioned above the right hip pocket, usually set at the same angle as the main pocket below it. It presents the cleanest, most discreet version of the ticket pocket, adding a period detail without any additional bulk or volume. On a formal city suit the jetted ticket pocket signals Edwardian restraint and an appreciation for tailoring history.

options.label: Welt Ticket Pocket
options.description: The welt ticket pocket is a miniature version of the breast pocket's welt treatment: a single strip of cloth sits below a neat horizontal slit, creating a defined mouth without the covering of a flap. It is slightly more visible than the jetted version and has a warm, traditional English character that suits tweed and flannel suits particularly well. The welt stands proud of the opening, giving the pocket a little more definition and visual weight than the fully flush jetted option.

options.label: Italian Card Slot
options.description: The Italian card slot is a minimalist horizontal slit — barely more than a scored line in the cloth — positioned just above the right hip pocket, sized precisely to accept a business card or folded ticket without any welt, flap, or jet dressing the opening. Associated with clean-lined Italian ready-to-wear and bespoke tailoring in the Milanese tradition, it gives the jacket a modern, graphic quality where the pocket reads as a deliberate incision rather than an ornamental feature. Its restraint makes it especially appropriate on slim, structured city suits in fine worsted.

options.label: Formal Card Holder
options.description: The formal card holder is a discreet slip of fabric attached on the surface of the jacket above the right hip pocket, engineered to hold cards or a folded ticket without creating any pocket mouth in the outer cloth. It effectively eliminates the visual interruption of a traditional ticket pocket while preserving the function, and is the preferred solution on the most formal bespoke commissions — morning coats, court dress, and ceremonial frock coats — where even a small jetted opening would disturb an otherwise immaculate front.

options.label: RL Card Pocket

options.label: Flap 4.0 cm
options.description: The flap ticket pocket is a miniature version of the main hip pocket flap, sitting above it with a small rectangular flap matching the angle and proportion of the lower pocket for a coherent, unified appearance across the hip. It is the most casual and most visible ticket pocket option, projecting a country-sporting character that is particularly at home on tweed jackets and hacking coats. When all three pockets on the right side are cut in perfect proportion and alignment, the result is one of the defining signatures of the English hacking jacket.
