options.label: SB 1 Button
options.description: The single-button single-breasted jacket is the most rakish and minimal of all lounge coat configurations, featuring a solitary button set precisely at the natural waist. Popularised by Savile Row houses such as Huntsman and Richard Anderson, it creates an unbroken vertical line from chest to hem that elongates the silhouette and gives the jacket a distinctly bespoke character rarely seen in ready-to-wear. Because the lapel rolls all the way down to the single fastening, the chest is shown off generously, making it a dressier and more striking alternative to the two-button standard.

options.label: SB 2 Buttons
options.description: The two-button single-breasted jacket is the most widely worn and versatile configuration in modern tailoring, fastening at a point that falls between the natural waist and the navel. Its lapel rolls cleanly to the top button, producing a balanced chest opening that suits virtually every body type; fewer buttons also create a lengthening, slimming effect compared to three-button alternatives. It is equally at home in business dress, smart-casual contexts, and formal suiting, which has made it the global standard since the mid-twentieth century.

options.label: SB 3 Buttons
options.description: The three-button single-breasted jacket carries a more conservative and slightly formal character, with the lapel rolling to the top button and creating a higher, more enclosed chest line. All three buttons are intended to be functional, though the lower button is conventionally left undone, giving it a neat, structured silhouette well suited to taller frames or those who prefer a traditional English or American collegiate look. The style was especially dominant in early-2000s tailoring and retains a timeless, understated dignity when cut with precision.

options.label: SB 4 Buttons
options.description: Four button — vintage heritage.

options.label: SB 3 Roll 2
options.description: The 3-roll-2 jacket has three buttons on the front but is cut and pad-stitched so that the lapel naturally rolls past the top button, which remains hidden, and only the middle button fastens. This creates a softer, more relaxed chest opening than a true three-button jacket while retaining the depth of a longer roll line associated with Neapolitan and classic American Ivy-League tailoring. The rolled lapel adds a gentle curve and suppleness that signals sophisticated hand-canvas construction and is considered one of the most elegant expressions of the single-breasted form.

options.label: SB 4 Roll 3
options.description: The 4-roll-3 is an unusual and rare configuration where four buttons are placed on the front but the lapel is engineered to roll past the top button so that only the third button fastens, leaving a particularly long, elegant roll line and an open chest. This style is associated with mid-twentieth-century American tailoring — particularly Ivy League draped-cut jackets — and its deep lapel roll produces a relaxed, almost casual grandeur that contrasts with the formality implied by four buttons. It suits elongated, loose-fitting cuts and robust, high-roll canvassing.

options.label: SB 5 Hidden Button
options.description: The five-button fly-front jacket conceals all fastening buttons behind an overlapping placket of fabric, presenting a completely smooth, unbroken front panel with no visible buttons whatsoever. This construction is most commonly associated with formal evening wear and military-inspired dress coats, lending the jacket a supremely clean and architectural silhouette. The hidden placket demands a high standard of tailoring to lie perfectly flat, and it projects an air of quiet, understated formality that sets it apart from all conventional button arrangements.

options.label: SB 5 Buttons
options.description: Five buttons — fashion-forward statement.

options.label: DB 2 × 1
options.description: The DB 2×1 is the most minimal double-breasted configuration, showing only two visible buttons in a single column with just one that fastens, producing a streamlined and unexpectedly contemporary silhouette. Its deep, wide lapel and low fastening point create a dramatic chest opening more reminiscent of a sophisticated lounge coat than a conventional double-breasted suit. This configuration is rare outside bespoke and niche tailoring and appeals to those who want the sculptural presence of a double-breasted jacket without the visual weight of six buttons.

options.label: DB 4 × 1
options.description: The DB 4×1 presents four buttons — two rows of two — but fastens only at the single lower button, so the jacket falls open across a broad chest and the lapel rolls deeply from the shoulder to the fastening point. This low, casual fastening is the traditional configuration for double-breasted dinner jackets and imparts a relaxed, confident elegance compared to the symmetrical formality of the 4×2. The resulting silhouette is particularly flattering, with the long uninterrupted roll of the lapel drawing the eye upward and adding visual height.

options.label: DB 4 × 2
options.description: The DB 4×2 uses four buttons arranged in two columns, both of which fasten, giving a cleaner and more modern look than the traditional six-button double-breasted. The reduced button mass lowers the visual weight of the jacket and suits men who want the double-breasted shape but find the 6×2 too imposing; it also favours shorter or slimmer frames by avoiding excess vertical button lines. Considered the contemporary alternative to the classic, it pairs especially well with lean, fitted cuts and solid or subtly patterned fabrics.

options.label: DB 6 × 1
options.description: The DB 6×1 is the most sartorially dramatic of the double-breasted family, presenting six buttons in two columns of three but fastening at only the single lowest button, producing an exceptionally wide and open chest with a sweeping lapel that falls almost to the waist. Originating in French tailoring, this configuration is particularly well suited to shorter torsos because the deep lapel roll adds an elongating effect to the upper body. The wide-open front reveals the full length of the tie and waistcoat and is considered a statement of extreme elegance when perfectly executed.

options.label: DB 6 × 2
options.description: The DB 6×2 is the definitive classic double-breasted configuration, with six buttons in two columns of three and two functioning closures positioned symmetrically at mid-chest, creating the iconic strong, wide-shouldered silhouette most associated with power dressing and traditional English tailoring. The two rows of buttons give the jacket visual authority and a sense of formality that flatters broader or taller frames, while the wide lapels and deep overlap of the fronts add structure and warmth. It is simultaneously the most formal and the most historically canonical of all double-breasted styles.

options.label: DB 6 × 3
options.description: The DB 6×3 takes the classic six-button layout but makes all three rows on the working side functional, creating the most buttoned-up and formally closed of all double-breasted jackets with a tight, compact front that sits flush from chest to waist. This configuration was common in naval and military-influenced tailoring where a secure, fully closed front was practical, and it lends the jacket a disciplined, almost uniform-like authority. The closely spaced triple fastening produces a very structured silhouette that is well suited to peak-lapel styles and heavier suiting fabrics.

options.label: By Machine
options.description: Machine-stitched buttonholes — precise.

options.label: By Hand
options.description: Hand-stitched buttonholes — bespoke hallmark.

options.label: -2 cm
options.description: Narrow dart — slim through chest.

options.label: -3 cm
options.description: Very narrow dart — fashion-slim.

options.label: Standard
options.description: Standard chest dart position.

options.label: +2 cm
options.description: Wider dart — fuller chest accommodation.

options.label: +3 cm
options.description: Widest dart — broad chest.

options.label: Round Hem
options.description: Standard round hem finish.

options.label: Round Hem +0.6 cm
options.description: Slightly extended round hem.

options.label: Squared Bottom
options.description: Square-cut hem — clean geometric.

options.label: Small Curved Bottom
options.description: Subtle curve hem.

options.label: Large Curved Bottom
options.description: Generous curve hem.

options.label: DR Round Hem
options.description: DR-specification round hem.

options.label: None
options.description: No sleeve vent means the cuff hem is a plain, uninterrupted finish with no slit, no overlap, and no buttons — the sleeve simply ends at the wrist in a clean line. This is the most minimal and economical sleeve finish, common on unlined summer jackets and casual sportswear where the understatement is intentional. It allows the most flexibility in sleeve length alteration after the fact and lends the jacket a clean, modern sensibility devoid of traditional tailoring signifiers.

options.label: Mock / Show Vent
options.description: A mock or show vent is a purely decorative sleeve opening: the vent is cut and finished to appear functional, with buttons sewn neatly in a row, but the buttonholes are either blind-stitched closed or omitted so the sleeve cannot actually be unbuttoned. This construction gives the visual impression of a working cuff — projecting quality and attention to detail — while preserving the ease of alteration that a genuine functioning vent would compromise. It is the standard finish on most quality made-to-measure and high-end ready-to-wear jackets.

options.label: Functional (Working)
options.description: Functional working buttonholes — also known as surgeon's cuffs — are the definitive hallmark of true bespoke construction: each button on the sleeve cuff is backed by a hand-sewn buttonhole that actually opens, allowing the sleeve to be rolled back or unbuttoned entirely. The buttonholes are almost always hand-worked in a gimp thread, producing a dense, slightly raised finish with subtle colour-matched or contrasting thread. This detail is impossible to add after the fact, confirming to any knowledgeable observer that the jacket was made from scratch to the wearer's precise measurements.

options.label: Functional + Mock Vent
options.description: Functional plus mock vent combines working buttonholes at the cuff with one or two extra non-functional button positions beyond the operative ones — creating an elongated row of buttons that appears more dramatic than standard surgeon's cuffs while retaining genuine functionality at the critical buttons. This is a collector's detail, signalling both technical knowledge and a refined sense of sleeve proportion. It is occasionally seen on jackets with five or six-button sleeves where the uppermost positions are purely aesthetic.

options.label: Functional (No Buttonhole)
options.description: Functional with no buttonhole means the sleeve vent is cut and constructed as a true working opening — with the full placket, lining, and turnback construction of a bespoke cuff — but without the hand-worked buttonholes being cut through. The vent can be opened to roll the sleeve, secured when closed by a single interior snap or small hook. This option provides the structural honesty of a true vent while remaining easily alterable, a practical choice for clients who want bespoke character without the length-fixing consequences of open buttonholes.

options.label: Angled Vent Cuff
options.description: The angled vent cuff is cut with a slight diagonal slant — typically dropping at the outer edge — so that when the arm hangs naturally, the cuff line follows the pitch of the wrist rather than sitting perfectly horizontal. This tailoring detail is common in English and Italian bespoke houses and serves both an aesthetic and ergonomic function: it allows a cleaner drape of the sleeve at the arm's natural angle and often better reveals a shirt cuff when the elbow is slightly bent. The result is a more animated, dynamic end to the sleeve compared to the static formality of a square cut.

options.label: Square Cuff
options.description: The square cuff terminates in a perfectly flat, perpendicular cut across the sleeve end with no angle or rounding at the corners, giving the jacket a crisp, architectural finish. This clean geometric line reads as formal and precise, and it is often chosen for structured business suits where a sharp, definitive edge complements the jacket's overall tailored severity. While less common than the angled style in bespoke work, the square cuff has a bold modernist appeal and pairs well with contrasting button thread or functioning surgeon's cuffs.

options.label: Round Cuff
options.description: The round cuff features gently curved corners at the sleeve opening, softening the transition from sleeve to hand and giving a more relaxed, organic feel than square or angled alternatives. It is the most forgiving and fluid of the standard cuff shapes, well suited to softer-construction jackets, sport coats, and casual suits where a gentler silhouette is desired. The rounded corners also prevent the cuff edge from catching inside the sleeve lining when dressing, and the style is widely favoured by Italian and Neapolitan tailors who prize ease and natural drape above rigid formality.

options.label: British Turn-up
options.description: The British turn-up, or gauntlet cuff, consists of a wide folded-back band at the sleeve end — typically a separate piece of fabric — creating a prominent visible cuff that wraps the wrist like a glove gauntlet. This Edwardian detail fell out of mainstream fashion but was kept alive by Savile Row bespoke tailors and enjoyed a revival in the 1950s and 1960s, particularly on English-made suit jackets and dinner coats. It is a highly visible mark of custom craftsmanship and adds visual weight and luxury to the sleeve end.

options.label: Turn-back 3.5 cm
options.description: The 3.5 cm turnback — often called a kissing cuff — is a narrower folded-back sleeve finish where the inner cuff folds outward by precisely 3.5 centimetres, leaving the cuff edge resting just against or lightly touching the sleeve face below. This measured fold introduces a subtle, refined decorative detail that shows the lining or a contrast inner fabric, adding depth without the boldness of a full gauntlet, and is particularly popular in Neapolitan and Spanish bespoke traditions. The exactness of the measurement gives each jacket a distinctive, considered quality.

options.label: Turn-back 4.0 cm
options.description: The 4.0 cm turnback is a slightly wider version of the kissing/cocktail cuff, folding back a full four centimetres so that the reversed cuff band is visibly broader and more assertive on the wrist. The additional width makes the turned fabric more prominent in motion and allows more of the lining or facing fabric to show, which is a prized detail in bespoke work. This cuff width sits at the generous end of the turnback spectrum and is associated with a relaxed confidence, often favoured on sports jackets, odd coats, and evening wear where the sleeve end becomes a conscious focal point.

options.label: None
options.description: No sleeve buttons.

options.label: 1 Button
options.description: Single sleeve button — minimal.

options.label: 2 Buttons
options.description: Two buttons — understated.

options.label: 3 Buttons
options.description: Three buttons — casual standard.

options.label: 4 Buttons
options.description: Four buttons — traditional formal.

options.label: 5 Buttons
options.description: Five buttons — statement piece.

options.label: 6 Buttons
options.description: Six buttons — maximum formal.

options.label: No Kissing (Standard)
options.description: Standard gap between buttons — classic.

options.label: Kissing Buttons
options.description: Buttons touching — Italian bespoke hallmark.

options.label: Straight
options.description: Horizontal buttonholes — traditional standard.

options.label: Slanted
options.description: Diagonal buttonholes — Italian style.

options.label: No Vent
options.description: A ventless back presents an unbroken, clean horizontal line across the entire hem of the jacket — no slits, no flaps, nothing to interrupt the silhouette from behind. Most closely associated with Italian tailoring and tuxedos globally, it creates a sleek, close-fitting appearance that flatters a slim physique and reads as fashion-forward and elegant when standing. The trade-off is mobility: sitting or reaching forward causes the back to pull and bunch, making it the least practical option for anyone who spends significant time seated.

options.label: Center Vent
options.description: The center vent is a single vertical slit running up the center back seam, originally derived from equestrian and military tailoring where the split allowed a rider to sit comfortably in the saddle without straining the back of the coat. It is today most closely associated with American tailoring — relaxed, practical, and universally wearable — and sits between the formality of side vents and the slickness of no-vent. The single vent has the minor visual disadvantage of parting and revealing the seat when hands are placed in trouser pockets, but remains the most common construction globally for its simplicity and ease of wear.

options.label: Side Vents
options.description: Side vents — two symmetrical vertical slits at the rear side seams — are the hallmark of British tailoring and the most functionally satisfying of all vent options. They allow the jacket to open evenly on both sides when the wearer sits or reaches into trouser pockets, meaning the back drapes flat and uninterrupted whether standing or moving. The double vent is considered the most refined option in classic English dress, communicating an understanding of tailoring tradition without any overt showiness, and is the standard configuration on Savile Row bespoke.

options.label: Side Vents + Inner Belt
options.description: Side vents with an inner martingale belt combine the practical elegance of British double vents with a horizontal half-belt stitched across the back at the waist — the belt is left loose at the centre, adjustable with buttons at each end, allowing the wearer to cinch or release the jacket's waist for a more tailored silhouette. A loose martingale is the more sporting, country-house variant of this detail, found on hacking jackets, tweed coats, and cavalry-inspired tailoring. When cinched, it creates a dramatic waist suppression and clean back that is difficult to achieve through cut alone.

options.label: Side Vents + Fixed Belt
options.description: Side vents with a fixed half-belt share the same back architecture as the martingale variant but the half-belt is sewn down at both the centre and the edges — it is purely decorative, providing the visual presence of a waist strap without any adjustability. This detail is most commonly seen on military-inspired overcoats and officer-cut jackets, where the stitched-down belt creates a strong horizontal line across the back that emphasises waist definition and gives the garment a commanding, architecturally resolved quality. It is a bolder, more formal statement than a loose martingale, particularly effective on jackets with bold fabrics like herringbone or cavalry twill.

options.label: None
options.description: No elbow patch leaves the sleeve in its clean, unadorned state — the sleeve fabric runs uninterrupted from shoulder to cuff. This is the default for all formal suits and most business jackets, where any addition to the sleeve would read as incongruous with the garment's intent. The absence of patches keeps the line of the jacket pure and undistracted, allowing the cloth itself — its weave, its drape, its lustre — to be the sole visual statement of the sleeve.

options.label: Round Elbow Patch
options.description: A round elbow patch is a classic oval or circular panel of leather, suede, or matching fabric stitched over the elbow point of the sleeve, providing reinforcement at the precise location where repeated flexion and abrasion wear through cloth first. On tweed, flannel, and corduroy sport coats this detail carries rich associations with country pursuits, academic life, and a deliberately relaxed form of elegance. In suede or tan leather against a heather tweed, the patch adds a warm tonal contrast that elevates the jacket from mere clothing to an object of considered character.

options.label: Round Sleeve Patch
options.description: A full round sleeve patch extends the reinforcing panel well beyond the elbow point to cover a significant portion of the lower sleeve — often running from just below the elbow down toward the cuff — providing protection against wear along the full forearm as well as the elbow. This elongated panel gives the jacket a distinctly sporting, almost military quality; on a tweed shooting jacket or a country hacking coat it reads as entirely purposeful and authentic. On a more dressy fabric it becomes a bold, statement-making sartorial choice.

options.label: None
options.description: No external decorative detail.

options.label: Front Chest Horizontal Button Loop
options.description: Flat button loop on front chest.

options.label: Front Chest Slanted Button Loop
options.description: Slanted button loop on front chest.

options.label: Standard Machine
options.description: A standard machine-made jacket relies entirely on industrial sewing machines for all seams, canvas attachment, and finishing, producing consistent and precise results at higher speed and lower cost than hand finishing. Long structural seams — back, side, sleeves — are done by machine in virtually all jackets including bespoke, as the straight length does not benefit from hand work. The overall character is uniform and neat, but lacks the subtle organic variation and flex of hand-sewn construction, and the jacket's canvas is typically fused or machine-padded rather than shaped by hand.

options.label: Individual Handmade Parts
options.description: A jacket with individual handmade parts combines machine sewing for long structural seams with hand work concentrated on the areas where it matters most: pad-stitching the lapels and collar canvas, hand-setting the sleeves into the armhole, hand-finishing the buttonholes, and hand-attaching the lining at its edges. This hybrid approach is the norm for the majority of high-quality bespoke and premium made-to-measure houses and delivers much of the softness, shape retention, and sartorial character of full handmade at a more practical time investment. It is the standard most discerning wearers associate with the term 'bespoke.'

options.label: Full Hand-made
options.description: A fully hand-sewn jacket is the apex of tailoring craft, where every stitch from canvas padding to the final slip-stitching of the lining hem is executed by hand, typically requiring over 60–80 hours of a master tailor's labour. The result is a garment with an incomparably fluid drape — the canvas is moulded through thousands of tiny pad stitches to the precise curvature of the wearer's chest, and every seam has the natural flexibility that only hand-sewn thread can provide. Beyond function, full handwork leaves visible traces of the maker's hand — slightly irregular stitch spacing, the organic roll of the lapel — that are considered the highest form of sartorial beauty.

options.label: None
options.description: When no decorative handmade lines are specified, the exterior of the jacket shows no visible hand-stitching on its seams, edges, or lapels — the construction reads as entirely clean and machine-finished. This is the appropriate choice for formal business suits and any context where an unadorned, austere surface is preferred, allowing the cloth itself, the cut, and any pick stitching to carry the visual interest without the additional artisan texture of exposed pad stitches. The absence of visible hand lines does not imply lower construction quality — the jacket may still be fully hand-canvassed internally.

options.label: Shoulder Seam Only
options.description: Decorative hand stitching limited to the shoulder seam places a single visible line of pad or pick stitching along the back of the shoulder where the sleeve head is set into the body, a location regarded as one of the most technically demanding in tailoring. This restrained placement signals hand craftsmanship without overwhelming the jacket's overall surface, and the slight unevenness of hand stitching at the shoulder gives the garment a living quality impossible to replicate by machine. It is a subtle, connoisseur's detail, more visible from behind and in motion than from the front.

options.label: Shoulder + Sleeve/Back Vent
options.description: This option extends visible hand-stitch lines to both the shoulder seam and the sleeve and back vent edges, framing the key structural boundaries of the jacket with a continuous decorative thread. The vent edges in particular benefit from hand finishing as it prevents the cloth from rolling outward and keeps the vent lying flat and crisp, while adding a textured border that elevates the jacket's rear view. The combination of shoulder and vent stitching creates a coherent framework of hand detail that reads as subtly artisan without drawing attention away from the cloth.

options.label: Full — Shoulder, Vents, Chest Pocket, Buttonholes
options.description: Full decorative handmade lines encompass the shoulder seam, both sleeve and back vents, the chest pocket edges, and all buttonholes, wrapping the jacket in a continuous signature of human craftsmanship that makes the construction process entirely legible from the outside. Each line of hand stitching serves the dual purpose of reinforcing the underlying structure and advertising the tailor's skill, as perfectly executed hand stitches at buttonholes, pocket lips, and vent edges are among the most admired details in sartorial culture. This is the full expression of the handmade tradition and belongs at the summit of bespoke tailoring.

options.label: Regular Machine Sewing
options.description: Regular machine sewing attaches buttons with a rapid, automated stitch pattern — typically a parallel-row attachment through all four holes — executed by an industrial buttonhole machine that creates a perfectly symmetrical, even thread pattern on every button. The result is neat and functional but visually uniform and lacking the slight organic variation that distinguishes hand-sewn buttons; it also typically omits the thread shank that lifts the button off the cloth to accommodate the buttonhole thickness. Machine sewing is the standard for ready-to-wear and much of made-to-measure production and is perfectly serviceable for everyday suits.

options.label: Crossing Stitch by Hand (X)
options.description: The cross-stitch (X) hand-sewn button uses thread to pass diagonally through the button holes twice in opposite directions, forming a visible X pattern on the button face that identifies the work as done by hand. This is one of the most traditional Savile Row attachment methods and is described in detail by tailors such as those at Huntsman: each pass is made through the cloth and gathered with a shank before the thread is wound and knotted off. The X pattern is a longstanding bespoke hallmark and, unlike machine-sewn buttons, the resulting attachment has a natural flex that moves with the cloth.

options.label: "↓" Stitch by Hand
options.description: The arrow-down (chevron) hand stitch sews the button with thread arranged so that the pairs of passes form a downward-pointing V or arrow shape when viewed from the front, a decorative variant on standard parallel attachment. Like other hand-sewn styles, it is executed with a thicker thread on a hand needle and includes a wound shank at the base of the button to give it the correct stand-off height from the cloth. The arrow pattern reads as a more assertive, graphic version of the equals stitch and has a confident, almost heraldic quality that suits bold button materials such as horn or corozo.

options.label: "=" Stitch by Hand
options.description: The equals-stitch (=) hand-sewn button attaches with thread running in two clean parallel rows across the four holes — similar to machine parallel sewing but executed entirely by hand — so that the surface of the button shows two neat horizontal lines of thread. Because the stitches are placed by hand with a thick buttonhole twist or silk thread, the slight irregularity and tighter twist of the shank are immediately distinguishable from machine work even though the pattern itself looks simple. This understated style suits formal business suits where the button attachment should be noticed by knowledgeable observers rather than casual viewers.

options.label: "□" Stitch by Hand
options.description: The square-stitch (□) hand-sewn button forms a full four-sided rectangular frame with the thread by passing diagonally and horizontally through all four holes in sequence, creating a small square of thread that encircles the button holes rather than simply crossing or running parallel. This technically demanding and highly decorative pattern is one of the rarest and most admired in bespoke tailoring, requiring a skilled hand to tension each pass consistently so the square sits flat and symmetrical. It signals maximum attention to the otherwise overlooked detail of button attachment and is best appreciated on plain smooth buttons in corozo, horn, or mother-of-pearl.

options.label: Button Under Layer by Hand
options.description: The button-under-layer technique places a small flat backing button on the inside of the cloth directly beneath each front button, connected through the fabric by the same thread, so that the outer button never tears the cloth however much strain is placed on the fastening. This is a mark of the highest-quality bespoke construction and was standard practice on Savile Row for heavy-duty working jackets, riding coats, and any garment expected to endure repeated forceful buttoning under outergarments. The backing button distributes load over a larger cloth area and is invisible from the outside but immediately legible as fine workmanship when the jacket is examined from within.

options.label: By Machine
options.description: Machine-stitched sleeve buttonholes — precise.

options.label: By Hand
options.description: Hand-stitched sleeve buttonholes — bespoke mark.

options.label: None
options.description: The absence of pick stitching leaves the jacket's lapel, collar, and pocket edges with a smooth, unbroken surface that draws no attention to the seam lines and presents the cloth in its purest, most unadorned form. This is the preferred choice for very formal business and dress suits where the design language is one of restraint, as well as for highly textured fabrics — bouclé, herringbone, or prominent tweed — where the weave itself provides sufficient visual interest. Without any edge stitch, the jacket's silhouette is defined purely by its cut and canvas.

options.label: 0.15 cm Machine
options.description: A 0.15 cm machine pick stitch runs very close to the edge of the lapel, collar, and pocket flaps, leaving a fine, almost invisible line that mimics the appearance of fine hand work while being produced rapidly and uniformly by machine. At this narrow distance from the edge, the stitch is nearly indistinguishable from the finest hand pick stitch to the untrained eye, and it reinforces the edges neatly without drawing attention to itself. It is the most popular machine pick-stitch specification for quality ready-to-wear and many made-to-measure tailors seeking a refined appearance with consistent output.

options.label: 0.6 cm Machine
options.description: At 0.6 cm, the machine pick stitch is set further from the edge and becomes more clearly legible as a decorative element — a visible line that frames the lapel and pocket edges and contributes texture and depth to the jacket's surface. This bolder stitch distance sits between the invisible and the strongly decorative, and it is widely used on smart-casual jackets, blazers, and sport coats where a degree of visible craftsmanship is desired. Because it is machine-made, the stitching is perfectly even and regular, which can read as polished and intentional rather than artisan.

options.label: Top Stitch 0.6 cm
options.description: Top stitching at 0.6 cm runs a single continuous thread parallel to the edge on the outside face of the lapel or collar, clearly visible as a neat accent line that both reinforces the edges and adds a sporty or casual decorative quality. Unlike pick stitching — which uses a stitch that catches the under-fabric with small buried loops — top stitching is an unbroken running stitch that lies flat on the surface, giving a more uniform and graphic appearance. It is well suited to casual sport coats, field jackets, and tweed blazers where a rugged, outdoorsy sartorial character is desired.

options.label: Top Stitch 4 cm from Edge
options.description: A top stitch set 4 cm back from the edge is a bold, architectural detail that creates a wide parallel inset border visible across the full expanse of the lapel face — more channel quilting than conventional pick stitching. This dramatic placement draws the eye along the full geometry of the lapel and collar, framing the jacket's front as a deliberate design feature rather than a subtle finishing touch. It is associated with military-inspired tailoring, structured overcoats, and fashion-forward bespoke commissions where the jacket's construction is meant to be read as part of its aesthetic.

options.label: 0.15 cm by Hand
options.description: Hand pick stitching at 0.15 cm is the most traditional and prized of all edge-finishing techniques: a tailor runs a needle through the underside of the facing and catches just a single thread on the surface at intervals of approximately 1.5 mm, producing a delicate broken line that no machine can truly replicate. The minute irregularities in spacing and thread tension that result from the human hand give the edge a living, textured quality that rewards close examination, and this is the definitive mark of a Savile Row or Neapolitan bespoke jacket. It takes considerable skill and time but results in an edge that is both perfectly sharp and softly organic.

options.label: 0.6 cm by Hand
options.description: A 0.6 cm hand pick stitch is set further from the edge and is individually pierced by hand, producing a clearly visible row of deliberate, evenly spaced surface stitches that form one of the most recognisable decorative signatures in high tailoring. Known as a 'swelled edge' in English tailoring tradition, this detail was a staple of Anthony Sinclair's work for Sean Connery's James Bond suits and is closely associated with the clean-cut English bespoke aesthetic. The slightly wider stitch distance makes each individual pick more visible and gives the lapel a bolder, more sporting character than the near-invisible 0.15 cm version.

options.label: Double (0.15 + 0.6 cm)
options.description: Double pick stitching runs two parallel rows along the lapel and collar edges simultaneously — one at 0.15 cm and a second at 0.6 cm — creating a layered, compound border that is uniquely rich and visible. This combination originated in English bespoke tailoring as a way to combine the structural security of a close edge stitch with the decorative bravado of a wider visible stitch, and the two lines together produce a framing effect that makes the jacket's edges read almost like a fine binding. It is a pronounced statement of craft and is well suited to sport coats and casual bespoke commissions where ornament is embraced.

options.label: Lapel, Collar, Front, Pocket, Sleeve Vent
options.description: Standard coverage — all key visible edges.

options.label: + Back Vent
options.description: Standard coverage plus back vent.

options.label: All Seams
options.description: Complete pick stitching on every seam.

options.label: No Contrast
options.description: When no contrast fabric is specified, the collar, lapels, and all visible panels of the jacket are cut from the same cloth as the body, maintaining a unified, single-material appearance that lets the cut and cloth speak without distraction. This is the standard configuration for all business and formal suiting and is appropriate whenever the goal is a coherent, authoritative silhouette rather than an exploration of material contrast. The absence of contrast is itself a design choice that communicates restraint, tradition, and a confidence in the quality of the primary cloth.

options.label: Top Collar
options.description: A contrast top collar replaces the standard matching collar cloth with a different material — most commonly a velvet, a contrasting colour, or a contrasting texture — creating a clearly visible band of alternative fabric at the back of the neck and along the undercollar seam where it frames the face. The velvet collar in particular has a long history in formal English dress, seen on chesterfield overcoats and Edwardian dress coats, and its addition to a suit jacket elevates the garment from purely functional tailoring to a statement of aesthetic refinement. Even a tonal contrast — for example, a dark grey velvet on a charcoal wool suit — adds depth and dimensionality at the jacket's highest and most visible point.

options.label: Lapel
options.description: Contrast lapel facings replace the outer cloth on both lapel faces with a different material — typically a contrasting colour, weight, or texture such as a matte wool against a tweed body — creating a visual frame around the jacket's chest opening that draws the eye to the lapel shape. This is a device used in both fashion-forward bespoke commissions and in traditional sporting jackets, where a plain solid-colour lapel on a patterned body (or vice versa) can read the shape of the lapel more clearly. Well executed, it requires careful pattern matching at the lapel seam and demands precise canvassing to ensure the facing lies flat without puckering.

options.label: Satin Piping on Lapel
options.description: Satin piping on the lapel applies a narrow ribbon or welt of high-lustre satin precisely along the lapel roll edge, creating a thin reflective line that traces the geometry of the lapel and collar with evening-wear elegance. This detail sits between a full satin facing and a plain cloth lapel, introducing just enough sheen to signal formality without converting the jacket into a conventional dinner suit. It is a refined, unusual choice particularly associated with bespoke evening wear, black-tie alternatives, and the overlap between formal suiting and theatrical dress, and it catches light beautifully in low-lit dining rooms and event spaces.

options.label: None
options.description: Omitting the chest pocket entirely produces the cleanest, most austere front on a jacket, erasing any visual interruption across the chest. Historically associated with tailcoats and the most elevated forms of formalwear, it was considered the hallmark of true ceremonial dress. In modern bespoke tailoring it is chosen on double-breasted dinner suits or highly formal morning coats where a boutonnière replaces the pocket square as the only chest ornament.

options.label: Full Lining
options.description: A fully lined jacket has its entire interior — back, sides, front panels, and sleeves — covered with a smooth lining fabric, typically Bemberg cupro or silk, which makes the jacket slip effortlessly over a shirt, helps it retain its pressed shape, and finishes the interior to a uniform standard. Full lining is the default for formal suits, winter-weight cloths, and any jacket intended for frequent wear in professional settings, as it prolongs the life of the outer cloth by reducing friction. The lining also provides an opportunity to introduce colour, pattern, or a monogrammed label as a hidden personal statement.

options.label: Half Lining
options.description: A half-lined jacket retains lining on the front panels and the upper half of the back but leaves the lower back unlined, exposing the reverse of the fabric or a neat turned-and-stitched seam finish. This construction substantially reduces the jacket's weight and improves ventilation across the back — where body heat accumulates most — without sacrificing the easy-donning comfort of lined front panels. It is the preferred choice for medium-weight spring and autumn suits and for bespoke clients who want a single jacket to serve across a broad range of temperatures.

options.label: Quarter Lining
options.description: Quarter lining restricts lining coverage to only the top portion of the back and the sleeve tubes, leaving the lower back and the under-arms entirely unlined and finished in the cloth itself. This is the cooler, more Southern-Italian approach to lining, commonly specified for linen, cotton, and lightweight fresco summer jackets where maximum breathability is the priority. The exposed interior seams must be finished to a high standard — typically hand-felled — which paradoxically makes a quarter-lined jacket more demanding and expensive to produce than a fully lined one.

options.label: Unlined
options.description: An entirely unlined jacket is the ultimate expression of lightweight, warm-weather tailoring, with nothing between the outer cloth and the wearer's skin but the jacket's canvas and seam allowances, all of which must be finished to a standard fit for open inspection. The absence of lining allows the construction — canvassing, seam edges, inner pockets — to be seen and examined, demanding higher craftsmanship and making the garment breathe maximally. Unlined jackets hang with a particularly clean, soft drape because there is no lining resistance, and they are the preferred choice for resort, summer, and casual bespoke commissions.

options.label: Matching to Fabric
options.description: A matching lining is cut from a fabric whose tone, texture, or pattern coordinates closely with the outer cloth — for example, a grey flannel suit lined in a charcoal Bemberg cupro — so that when the jacket flares open in motion, the interior reads as a deliberate continuation rather than a contrasting surprise. This choice is typically favoured for conservative formal suiting where understatement is the brief, and it gives the jacket a unified, monolithic character that reads as sober and assured. The matching lining also tends to be the least distracting choice for fabrics with strong surface character such as windowpane check or pinstripe.

options.label: Navy Solid
options.description: A navy solid lining is among the most versatile and handsome interior choices across all suit colours — it pairs naturally with grey, charcoal, and brown outer fabrics while introducing a subtle nautical richness that reads confidently when the jacket lapels fall open. Navy Bemberg or silk lining gives the interior a clean, formal character that contrasts without clashing, making it a safe upgrade from a matching lining for anyone wanting a quiet personality note inside their suit. It is widely offered by English and Italian bespoke houses as the standard 'house lining' recommendation for business suiting.

options.label: Black
options.description: Black lining is the natural choice for formal evening wear — tuxedos, dinner suits, and black-tie jackets — where it creates a seamlessly dark interior that reinforces the jacket's dressiest register. Against lighter fabrics such as ivory or midnight blue it provides a crisp, high-contrast flash when the jacket moves, and for dark wool suits it produces a unified, solemn depth that suits both City formality and evening elegance. Black linings are also practical, as they are more resistant to visible soiling and fading than pale or patterned alternatives.

options.label: Cream / Ivory
options.description: A cream or ivory lining introduces warmth and a sense of historic luxury to the jacket interior, evoking the soft interiors of Edwardian dress coats and vintage English suiting from the golden age of bespoke tailoring. It is particularly effective in dark navy, charcoal, or black outer fabrics where the pale interior creates a luminous flash of contrast when the jacket opens, and it suits formal occasions where the wearer wants understated opulence rather than bold colour. Cream Bemberg or silk has a slightly warmer, more aged feeling than pure white and flatters virtually all skin tones when glimpsed at the lining edge.

options.label: Dark Charcoal
options.description: Dark charcoal lining occupies the tonal space between black and navy, offering a sober, serious interior that blends effortlessly with grey, charcoal, and dark blue outer fabrics without the stark formality of pure black. It is particularly popular in English business suiting where the overall look is one of controlled authority, and the near-neutral dark tone means the lining never distracts from the cloth's surface. Charcoal Bemberg cupro also ages gracefully without showing the yellowing or fading that lighter linings can develop over time.

options.label: Burgundy
options.description: Burgundy lining is one of the most beloved and enduring colour choices in bespoke tailoring, introducing a warm, vinous richness to the jacket interior that pairs magnificently with navy, grey, and brown outer fabrics. When the jacket opens, a flash of deep red-purple lining signals confidence and a refined taste for quiet drama, and the colour has strong associations with Savile Row heritage, private clubs, and old English luxury. It is perhaps the single most popular 'statement lining' choice for classic suits and is flattering across a wide range of complexions.

options.label: Gold Paisley Pattern
options.description: A gold paisley lining is among the most baroque and decorative options available to the bespoke client, featuring the ancient teardrop-and-leaf motif in rich amber and gold tones woven into silk or Jacquard. Paisley in tailoring lining has roots in nineteenth-century aristocratic dress and carries connotations of cosmopolitan aestheticism and a relaxed relationship with historical ornament. When glimpsed as the jacket swings open, it delivers a theatrical internal flourish that transforms a sober exterior into a private declaration of personality, and it is frequently specified for dinner jackets, opera coats, and personality-forward bespoke commissions.
